teaching_card.title: Modifier Placement (Easy)
teaching_card.body: An introductory phrase must modify the noun that immediately follows it. If it doesn't, the modifier 'dangles' and the sentence says something illogical. Place the right noun right after the phrase.
key_points: Put the modified noun right after the phrase.
Check that the opening phrase logically fits that noun.

screens.title: Modifiers need the right neighbor
screens.body: An opening descriptive phrase attaches to the noun that comes right after the comma. If that noun can't logically do the action, the modifier 'dangles' and the sentence says something absurd.
key_points: The noun after the comma must fit the opening phrase.

screens.title: Spot the absurdity
screens.body: Read the opening phrase, then ask 'who or what is doing this?' The answer must be the first word after the comma. 'Walking to school, the rain soaked us' wrongly says the rain was walking.
key_points: Name the doer, then check it's right after the comma.
Fix by putting the right noun first (or rewriting).

screens.title: Try the moves
screens.body: 'Having studied all night, the exam felt easy' — the exam didn't study. The right subject is the student: 'Having studied all night, she found the exam easy.'
screens.worked_example: Opening phrase about a person -> a person must follow the comma.
